sourceMovie.title: Halloween
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Halloween (1978) as a horror theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Halloween
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Halloween's horror stayed with you, Halloween (2018) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The thriller through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Scream VI
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Halloween excels in horror, Scream VI (2023) doubles down on thriller to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Halloween Kills
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Halloween Kills (2021) is the natural next step after Halloween: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and thriller elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Halloween II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Halloween who respond to its horror will find Halloween II (1981) immediately familiar. Both films share a thriller sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Haunt
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Halloween fans keep landing on Haunt (2019) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Jason Lives - Friday the 13th Part VI
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Jason Lives - Friday the 13th Part VI draws from the same horror well as Halloween, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1986 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Scream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Scream (1996) captures the horror appeal of Halloween without simply imitating it. The crime streak running through both films is what puts Scream at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Scream 4
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of Halloween — that dark edge, the mystery — translates cleanly into Scream 4 (2011). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Child's Play
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Child's Play (1988) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Halloween rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: The Texas Chainsaw Massacre
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Texas Chainsaw Massacre (2003) is the kind of film Halloween fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

faq.question: What should I watch after Halloween?
faq.answer: Try Halloween, Scream VI, Halloween Kills—each shares DNA with Halloween while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Halloween?
faq.answer: Halloween (1978) is best described as Horror, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Halloween part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Halloween Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
